title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Mrs. Habert suggests that M. Poirot advise the group on recent events and recommends calling the police. This sparks an animated discussion, with Genevieve protesting the idea of involving the police. During the debate, Poirot presents Sally Finch with a pair of evening shoes, explaining that he deduced the missing shoe had been taken to Baker Street station's lost and found. He reasons that a thief would likely hide a single shoe on public transport during rush hour rather than attempting to destroy it or sell it. While Nigel and Patricia defend Nigel against accusations of the theft, Poirot observes the agitated Len Bateson and the other students. Poirot notes his delicate position as a guest and states he will only discuss the "nuisance" if the group invites him to do so. After Archibald Bletchley suggests a democratic vote, Sally Finch and Nigel urge Poirot to speak. Poirot then officially recommends that Mrs. Haberdashers or Mrs. Nicoletti call the police immediately.

Following the suggestion, Poirot is escorted to a private sitting room by Mrs. Haberdashers. She expresses anxiety and agrees with his recommendation, though she wishes he had been more discreet to avoid a scandal. The conversation is interrupted by Colin McNaughton, a graduate student in psychiatry and psychology. Colin criticizes Poirot’s methods as outdated and narrow, arguing that crime should be understood through psychological motivation rather than just legal guilt and punishment. After a brief disagreement with Mrs. Haberdashers, Colin begins to explain his perspective to Poirot, using the theft of Sally Finch's shoe as an example of a "Cinderella complex."
